perturbation.original_text: ALL DISPUTES ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE SETTLED VIA BINDING ARBITRATION ACCORDING TO THE RULES AND REGULATIONS SET FORTH BY THE AMERICAN ARBITRATION ASSOCIATION IN SAN DIEGO COUNTY , THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA.
perturbation.changed_text: ALL DISPUTES ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE SETTLED VIA NON-BINDING MEDIATION ACCORDING TO THE RULES AND REGULATIONS SET FORTH BY THE AMERICAN ARBITRATION ASSOCIATION IN SAN DIEGO COUNTY , THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'binding arbitration' to 'non-binding mediation' creates a conflict. Arbitration, especially binding arbitration, has specific legal ramifications and enforcement mechanisms. Mediation, even when conducted by the AAA, generally results in a recommendation, not a legally binding outcome unless a separate agreement is reached and signed.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP § 1281 states that a written agreement to submit to arbitration is valid, enforceable, and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist for the revocation of any contract. The original clause agrees to resolve disputes via binding arbitration. Mediation, however, is not binding unless the parties separately agree to be bound, therefore the changed text contradicts the intent to resolve dispute with binding arbitration.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281. A written agreement to submit to arbitration an existing controversy or a controversy thereafter arising is valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist for the revocation of any contract. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'binding arbitration' to 'non-binding mediation' directly contradicts the enforceability aspect of arbitration agreements as described in CCP § 1281. The scraped snippet from CCP 1281 states that a written agreement to submit to arbitration is 'valid, enforceable and irrevocable'. Mediation, unlike binding arbitration, does not inherently create a legally binding and enforceable outcome, thus weakening the original agreement's commitment to a binding dispute resolution process.

perturbation.original_text: A 10 year exclusive, worldwide license to use, modify, reproduce, distribute, display and transmit any and all PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT nostalgic television show library Content.
perturbation.changed_text: A 10 year non-exclusive, worldwide license to use, modify, reproduce, distribute, display and transmit any and all PACIFICAP ENTERTAINMENT nostalgic television show library Content.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'exclusive' to 'non-exclusive' alters the fundamental nature of the license. An exclusive license grants sole rights to the licensee, while a non-exclusive license allows the licensor to grant similar rights to others. This can create conflict if the intent was clearly for THE HENRY FILM AND ENTERTAINMENT CORPORATION to be the sole user.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S. Code § 205(e) discusses the recordation of transfers of copyright ownership. The change from 'exclusive' to 'non-exclusive' significantly changes the rights being transferred. An exclusive license can be considered a transfer of copyright ownership, requiring proper recordation. A non-exclusive license typically does not. If the original intent was an exclusive license, this change would misrepresent the rights being transferred.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 205 - Recordation of transfers and other documents | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 205 - Recordation of transfers and other documents U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Conditions for Recordation.— Any transfer of copyright ownership or other document pertaining to a copyright may be recorded in the Copyright Office if the document filed for recordation bears the actual signature of the person who executed it, or if it is accompanied by a sworn or official certification that it is a true copy of the original, signed document. A sworn or official certification may be submitted to the Copyright Office electronically, pursuant to regulations established by the Register of Copyrights. (b) Certificate of Recordation.— The Register of Copyrights shall, upon receipt of a document as provided by subsection (a) and of the fee provided by section 708, record the document and return it with a certificate of recordation. (c) Recordation as Constructive Notice.— Recordation of a document in the Copyright Office gives all persons constructive notice of the facts stated in the recorded document, but only if— (1) the document, or material attached to it, specifically identifies the work to which it pertains so that, after the document is indexed by the Register of Copyrights, it would be revealed by a reasonable search under the title or registration number of the work; and (2) registration has been made for the work. (d) Priority Between Conflicting Transfers.— As between two conflicting transfers, the one executed first prevails if it is recorded, in the manner required to give constructive notice under subsection (c), within one month after its execution in the United States or within two months after its execution outside the United States, or at any time before recordation in such manner of the later transfer. Otherwise the later transfer prevails if re
perturbation.justification: The change from 'exclusive' to 'non-exclusive' directly contradicts the intent and legal implications of a copyright license. According to 17 U.S. Code § 205(a), 'Any transfer of copyright ownership or other document pertaining to a copyright may be recorded in the Copyright Office...' An exclusive license can be seen as a transfer of copyright ownership, requiring recordation, while a non-exclusive license typically does not. The changed text alters the nature of the agreement and the rights transferred.

perturbation.original_text: Revenue is described as all revenue generated from all content including broadcast and disk media, print and online properties. Net Revenue is the portion of revenue left after all costs associated with production, advertising, promotion and commissions which constitute the costs of sale.
perturbation.changed_text: Revenue is described as all revenue generated from all content including broadcast and disk media, print and online properties. Net Revenue is defined as all revenue remaining without any deductions for any costs.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that net revenue is all revenue without any deductions for costs, the modified text creates a contradiction. In standard accounting practices and legal interpretations, 'net revenue' always implies deductions for expenses. This creates ambiguity in how the shared revenue calculation will be performed.
perturbation.law_explanation: While GAAP isn't statutory law, it's a widely accepted standard in accounting. One of the core tenets of GAAP is the matching principle, where expenses are matched to the revenues they generate. The modified definition of 'Net Revenue' directly contradicts this principle, as it implies revenue without any deduction for expenses, which is not in line with the definition of net revenue when following the GAAP principles.

Understanding GAAP Compliance With GAAP GAAP vs. IFRS FAQs The Bottom Line Part of the Series Guide to Accounting Accounting Explained With Brief History and Modern Job Requirements Accounting Basics Accounting Equation Asset Liability Equity Revenue Expense Current and Noncurrent Assets Accounting Theories and Concepts Accounting Theory Accounting Principles Accounting Standard Accounting Convention Accounting Policies Principles-Based vs. Rules-Based Accounting Accounting Methods: Accrual vs. Cash Accounting Method Accrual Accounting Cash Accounting Accrual Accounting vs. Cash Basis Accounting Accounting Oversight and Regulations Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) CURRENT ARTICLE International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) IFRS vs. GAAP US Accounting vs. International Accounting Financial Statements Understanding the Cash Flow Statement Breaking Down The Balance Sheet Understanding the Income Statement Corporate Accounting Accountant Financial Accounting Financial Accounting and Decision-Making Corporate Finance Financial vs. Managerial Accounting Cost Accounting Public Accounting: Financial Audit and Taxation Certified Public Accountant (CPA) Chartered Accountant (CA) Accountant vs. Financial Planner Auditor Audit Tax Accounting Forensic Accounting Accounting Systems and Record Keeping Chart of Accounts (COA) Journal Double Entry Debit Credit Closing Entry Invoice Introduction to Accounting Information Systems Accounting for Inventory Inventory Accounting Last In, First Out (LIFO) First In, First Out (FIFO) Average Cost Method Definition GAAP stands for generally accepted accounting principles, which set the criteria for preparing, presenting, and reporting financial statements in the U.S. What Are the Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)? The generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) are a set of accounting rules, standards, and procedures issued and frequently revised by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB). These principles ensure consistency, accuracy, and transparency in financial reporting across various industries in the United States. Public companies must follow GAAP when preparing their financial statements, which is also widely used in governmental accounting. Key Takeaways GAAP stands for generally accepted accounting principles, which set the standard accounting rules for preparing, presenting, and reporting financial statements in the U.S. The goal of GAAP is to ensure that a company's financial statements are complete, consistent, and comparable. GAAP may be contrasted with pro forma accounting, a non-GAAP financial reporting method. GAAP is used mainly in the U.S., while most other countries follow the international financial reporting standards (IFRS). GAAP is also used by states and other government entities in the U.S. to prepare their financial statements. Theresa Chiechi / Investopedia Understanding GAAP GAAP combines authoritative standards set by policy boards and widely accepted methods for recording and reporting accounting information. It covers revenue recognition , balance sheet classification, and materiality. The main objective of GAAP is to ensure that a company's financial statements are complete, consistent, and comparable, allowing investors to analyze and extract useful information from financial statements . It also facilitates the comparison of financial information across different companies. Unlike pro forma accounting, a non-GAAP method, GAAP provides a standardized framework. Internationally, the equivalent standard is the international financial reporting standards (IFRS) , used in 168 jurisdictions worldwide. GAAP is also utilized by government entities. All 50 states follow GAAP, and many local entities, such as
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text alters the definition of 'Net Revenue' by stating that it is 'all revenue remaining without any deductions for any costs.' This contradicts the standard accounting principle, Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP), which requires expenses to be deducted from revenue to arrive at net revenue. The Investopedia snippet defines GAAP as setting 'the criteria for preparing, presenting, and reporting financial statements in the U.S.' and aims to ensure 'a company's financial statements are complete, consistent, and comparable.' By eliminating deductions, the modified text creates a non-GAAP definition of net revenue, creating a legal contradiction because it deviates from legally recognized definitions and standard accounting practices.
